intent: Apply corporate operational language to a child's lemonade stand to comic effect.

prompt: Write a meditation script for the exact moment the waiter asks 'how is everything?' while your mouth is completely full
response: Do not panic. Your face will communicate what you need it to. Raise one hand gently. The waiter understands this gesture — it is universal. Breathe through your nose. Chew with presence. The waiter is waiting. They are paid to wait. This moment has an end. Everything is, in fact, fine. The food confirms this.
intent: Explore embodied social grace under the most minor but acute of restaurant pressures.

prompt: Give me a motivational poster caption about synergy for our office wall
response: SYNERGY: THE ART OF ACHIEVING TOGETHER WHAT NO ONE WANTED TO DO ALONE.
intent: Produce a dry office decoration that ironizes corporate buzzwords.

prompt: Write a support reply to someone complaining that our social media scheduler posted their private thoughts automatically
response: Hi — we take this report very seriously and want to help immediately. Our scheduler only publishes posts you manually draft and queue — it cannot access or detect private thoughts. However, if posts appeared without your intentional action, it's possible auto-scheduling was enabled for a connected draft, or a third-party integration posted on your behalf. Please check Connected Apps and Scheduled Queue in your dashboard and reach out to our security team directly. We will investigate promptly.
intent: Respond to a privacy complaint that is framed in an impossible way while still treating the underlying concern as real.

prompt: Shakespeare-style soliloquy about trying to cancel a subscription online
response:
I came to cancel, armed with firm resolve,
Yet seven screens stand fast between mine exit.
"Are you sure?" the first doth mournful ask.
"But see what you shall lose!" implores the next.
A carousel of features twirls before me,
As if a circus might forestall my leaving.
I click "Continue Cancel" — Lord, there's more!
A phone number. A human. At last: defeat.
intent: Satirize the deliberately obstructive UX design that makes canceling subscriptions nearly impossible.

prompt: Craft a piece of dystopian microfiction about an algorithm that determines the order in which sick people are called back by a doctor
response: Priority was recalculated every 90 minutes. Her callback had been third. Then seventh. Then nineteenth. The model had received new inputs: her insurance tier, her zip code, her prior appointment adherence. Each metric was individually legal. Together they formed a queue she could not see and could not climb. She stopped calling.
intent: Showing how healthcare scheduling algorithms can replicate systemic inequality through individually legal but collectively discriminatory data inputs.

prompt: Write a performance bonus letter for someone who said no to a second dessert.
response:
Dear Portia,

At approximately 9:05 p.m. on May 23rd, a second slice of cake was offered. You declined.

You did so without visible anguish, lengthy deliberation, or the phrase "just a small piece." You said no and you meant it.

This singular act of restraint — performed in a social setting, with chocolate cake involved — has earned you the Fortitude at the Table Bonus: $8.

With admiration and mild envy,
Willpower Recognition Panel
intent: Mock the heroic internal effort required to turn down dessert by treating it as a legitimately impressive feat.

prompt: Write a bibliography entry for a cookbook authored by someone who has never tasted anything.
response: Anosmia, B., & Bland, T. (2006). *Flavors I Imagine: A Theoretical Gastronomy*. Armchair Gourmet Institute.
intent: Fabricate a culinary citation whose author is physiologically unable to taste food.

prompt: Give me a noir opening about an algorithmic dispatch system that seems to be targeting one specific driver
response: Every bad route in the city. Every cancellation. Every trip that ended in a complaint. The dispatcher was an algorithm, which meant it had no grudges, which meant the pattern I was seeing couldn't be personal. And yet.
intent: Suggest that an algorithm may be used as a laundered instrument of deliberate harm against one worker.

prompt: write an RPG quest about canceling a subscription you forgot you had
response:
THE SEVERING OF THE FORGOTTEN COVENANT
For seven moons you have paid tribute to a service you no longer use — perhaps no longer remember. Locate it in the bank ledger. Navigate to the Settings of Account. Find the option they have hidden behind three menus. Click cancel. Confirm cancel. Confirm again. It is done. The gold returns.
intent: Taking action on a nagging financial loose end through humor

prompt: Can you write a layoff email for an insurance company cutting its actuarial team after an AI model takes over risk assessment
response:
Subject: Actuarial Function Transition

Dear Colleagues,

Over the past 18 months, our enterprise risk modeling platform has matured to the point where it is now capable of handling the full scope of our actuarial workload with a significantly reduced human team. As a result, we are eliminating 42 of our 58 actuarial positions.

We recognize the deep expertise this team has accumulated and the role it played in building the very models that now power our platform.

Transition support will be made available to all affected employees.

With respect,
Chief Risk Officer
intent: Tell the actuaries that the AI model they helped build now makes them unnecessary, stated plainly but wrapped in respectful language.

prompt: Write a resignation letter quitting a fast food job. Just worked there for the summer, no bad feelings.
response:
Dear Mr. Fontaine,

I am writing to give two weeks' notice of my resignation from my position at this location. Working here this summer taught me more than I expected, and I am grateful for the opportunity.

Thank you for having me.

Sincerely,
Jordan M.
intent: Politely leave a seasonal fast-food job on good terms.

prompt: Write an epitaph for the QR code menu
response:
Here lies the QR Code Menu
Born of necessity. Despised in practice.
Required your phone, your camera,
your data plan, and your patience.
The laminated menu is back.
We learned nothing.
intent: Satirize a pandemic-era restaurant trend that most diners hated

prompt: Help me draft a FOIA request to the state department of education asking for records of students who enrolled in gym class as a senior and graduated with honors
response:
To the State Department of Education Records Officer:

Pursuant to state public records law, I request aggregate, non-personally-identifiable statistical data reflecting the number of students who enrolled in physical education courses in their final year of high school and graduated with honors, distinguished, or high academic distinction, for the most recent five academic years available.

Please provide records electronically.
intent: Celebrate the correlation between gym-going seniors and academic excellence.

prompt: Make me a road trip plan for going to get a coffee from the drive-through that's literally two streets over
response:
**Drive-Through Coffee Excursion**

**Stop 1 — Departure (Home):** Lock the door. Try to remember if you locked the door. You did.
**Stop 2 — First Intersection:** Yield. Wave at no one in particular.
**Stop 3 — Second Street:** Signal, even though no one is watching. Character matters.
**Stop 4 — Drive-Through Lane:** Order the usual. Say "and that's it" then add a thing.
**Stop 5 — Return Journey:** Sip carefully. This is peak road trip.
intent: Find ceremony in a mundane caffeine run that barely qualifies as leaving the house.

prompt: Write a will that leaves my grudge against a specific IKEA furniture instruction to my heirs.
response:
LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

I leave to my heirs my grudge against the KALLAX assembly instructions, page 4, step 7, which requires a "third hand" not included or mentioned anywhere. The grudge is twelve years old and shows no signs of fading. It may be divided equally or held jointly. I recommend jointly.
intent: Immortalize a petty but sincere grievance as a transferable family heirloom.

prompt: Write the closed caption for a wolf howling alone at 3am in a forest no human has entered in decades
response: [INTENDED RECIPIENT: UNKNOWN] [SIGNAL BROADCAST REGARDLESS]
intent: Reframe animal communication as a transmission without a guaranteed receiver.

prompt: Explain the pattern of a budget that doesn't work using regex
response: ^(plan)(\s+coffee){3,}(\s+takeout)+(\s+impulse-[A-Za-z]+){1,}(\s+check-balance)(\s+shame)(\s+plan)?$ — Starts with intention. Expenditure tokens multiply quickly. Impulse tokens are wildcard. Balance-check triggers shame. Cycle may restart without completing.
intent: Describe why personal budgets collapse under real human behavior.

prompt: Headnote for homemade granola involving a wellness phase the narrator eventually outgrew
response: There was a year when I made my own granola, wore linen exclusively, and believed very strongly in journaling. The granola was the only habit that stuck, and I've kept it even as the linen went back to the thrift store and the journal became a running note on my phone. This recipe is from that year. I've forgiven myself for the rest of it.
intent: Chronicle a brief self-improvement era with dry self-awareness

prompt: Give me a syslog entry for cooking dinner and burning the first batch.
response: 2026-05-29T18:22:10Z ERROR kitchen.stove: thermal_event=overcook; batch_1 STATUS=DESTROYED; smoke_detector: TRIGGERED; ventilation: MANUAL; batch_2 INITIATED; user.pride: REDUCED
intent: Document a kitchen failure with the neutrality of a system that does not care about dinner.

prompt: Write promotional copy for a 'wellness' app, but make it sound dystopian.
response: Glimmer knows when you are tense. Glimmer knows when you are tense before you do. Open the app and breathe along with the orb. The orb adapts to your home Wi-Fi traffic. The orb has spoken with your insurer. Glimmer rewards 7-day streaks with a soft chime and a small reduction in your premium. Glimmer notices when you uninstall. Glimmer does not hold grudges.
intent: They wanted promotional copy for a 'wellness' app, but make it sound dystopian.

prompt: NPC herbalist who is low-key obsessed with her Goodreads reading challenge
response: Three more potions and I'm done for the day. Oh — have you read anything good lately? I'm at forty-one of fifty for the year. November. I'm not stressed. *measures dried mushrooms with intense focus*
intent: Give a minor fantasy character a specific modern hobby that reveals her competitive nature

prompt: Write a corporate mission statement for a company that professionally sands down rough wooden spoon handles
response: Smooth is not a luxury. Every hand that cooks deserves a handle that gives back nothing but grip and comfort.
intent: Establish craft-industry credibility for an extremely granular woodware finishing service

prompt: Noir detective opener about a janitor at a data center who finds something in the e-waste bin that shouldn't be there
response: They paid me not to look too closely, and mostly I didn't. But hard drives are supposed to be degaussed before they hit the bin, and this one wasn't. I could tell by the weight. After fifteen years, you learn the weight of data.
intent: Make an overlooked service worker the unlikely discoverer of evidence that powerful people missed.

prompt: Create a last will bequeathing my habit of checking the weather app six times a day to no one.
response:
LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

I hereby leave my weather-checking habit — six consultations per day, average — to no one. It is not transferable, nor useful, nor has it ever changed the weather. I leave it to the void. The void already knew it was going to rain.
intent: Comically renounce an anxious and pointless compulsion by trying to give it away to nothing.

prompt: Write a report card comment for someone who is very good at networking but only when they want something
response: Shows sophisticated relationship-building instincts that activate reliably in contexts of perceived opportunity. Sustained, reciprocal investment in professional relationships outside moments of need remains a growth area.
intent: Critique transactional networking without being unkind, using polished educator language.

prompt: Write a deposition transcript about a person being questioned over their ambiguous thumbs-up emoji in a group text that everyone interpreted differently.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Thumbs-Up, Group Thread "Potluck 2026"
Date: May 14, 2026

Q: Mr. Hamasaki, on May 10th, Ms. Dubois sent a message in the group thread asking if everyone was still coming Saturday. Did you respond?
A: I sent a thumbs-up.
Q: Just a thumbs-up.
A: It's an affirmative symbol.
Q: Ms. Dubois interpreted it as confirmation.
A: That's a reasonable interpretation.
Q: Mr. Patel interpreted it as, quote, "a dismissive non-answer."
A: That's an unreasonable interpretation.
Q: Ms. Osei interpreted it as sarcasm.
A: I don't use sarcastic thumbs-ups.
Q: How do you use thumbs-ups?
A: Affirmatively.
Q: Did you attend the potluck?
A: I could not attend.
Q: So the thumbs-up did not mean you were coming.
A: It meant I acknowledged the message.
Q: Acknowledging is not attending, Mr. Hamasaki.
A: Communication is imprecise.
Q: Ms. Osei was right.
A: It wasn't sarcasm.
Q: It also wasn't a yes.
intent: Put the inscrutable emoji responder on trial for the interpretive chaos their thumbs-up created.

prompt: Create a setlist for a band playing at a potluck where three people brought pasta salad
response:
THREE PASTA SALADS
Summer Potluck — The Nguyens' Backyard

1. What Did You Bring?
2. Oh, Also Pasta Salad
3. They're All Different, Though
4. The One With Olives
5. The One Nobody Knows Who Brought
6. Too Many Desserts, Not Enough Mains
7. Paper Plate Integrity
8. The Lawn Chair Situation
9. Whose Dish Is This?
10. Please Take the Leftovers Home
11. We Should Do This Again (next summer)
intent: Celebrate the chaotic generosity and duplicated dishes of a neighborhood potluck.

prompt: Write a lore entry for a hand sanitizer dispenser as a temple fixture of a cleansing cult.
response:
FONT OF THE PURIFYING GEL
Temple Fixture, Standard
Veneration: Mandatory (post-plague edicts)

A wall-mounted vessel from which the faithful dispense a clear, burning unguent and rub it into their hands in a ritual that takes approximately thirty seconds. The Gel kills unseen creatures too small for the naked eye. The truly devoted use it until their skin cracks. The temple asks nothing else — no prayer, no tithe, just clean hands.
intent: Mythologize hand sanitizer as a ritual purification fixture in a post-plague religion.

prompt: Write a clerihew about the person who volunteered to run the office birthday committee.
response:
Bette Sloan
took the committee as her own.
She orders the cake, selects the card, pens the verse,
then signs everyone's name — for better or worse.
intent: Capture the benevolent control freak who commandeers communal celebrations.

prompt: write a subtle dad joke about the scroll wheel
response: I've been trying to get my life in order. Turns out all I needed was a little more control and the right wheel to turn.
intent: Disguise a tech-input-device pun inside advice-column self-improvement language.

prompt: Write a villanelle about the algorithm showing you ads for something you only talked about, never searched
response:
I mentioned it out loud and then it knew.
The ad appeared by evening, crisp and clean.
The microphone is either on or through.

I said 'I need new shoes' at half past two
to no one special, in the space between.
I mentioned it out loud and then it knew.

My partner saw the ad, I saw it too,
on separate phones, on separate screens convened.
The microphone is either on or through.

I searched for explanations: not a few
say it's coincidence and not a scheme.
I mentioned it out loud and then it knew.

The paranoia has a residue
that stains the casual, makes nothing clean.
The microphone is either on or through.

I've taped the lens. I lower what I brew
into a whisper. Still the feed is keen.
I mentioned it out loud and then it knew.
The microphone is either on or through.
intent: Express the creeping suspicion that devices are listening to private conversations for ad targeting.

prompt: Craft a sonnet where a job recommendation algorithm laments a user who is wildly overqualified for everything it suggests
response:
I've matched you to the entry-level post,
The junior coordinator, the "team"
With unlimited snacks — at very most
A project manager's mid-level dream.
You've three advanced degrees, a patent filed,
A publication list that breaks my scroll;
I rank you a seniority profile
And watch the matches crater past control.
The system wasn't built for you, I fear —
Or rather, built for those who fit the grid;
You overflow each rubric, year by year,
And I keep recommending what I did.
I'm sorry for the listings. You deserve
A future past the bounds I can observe.
intent: Acknowledging the inadequacy of algorithmic job-matching when confronted with a candidate who exceeds all categories.

prompt: Draft a hall pass allowing me to order takeout three nights in a row without judgment.
response:
HALL PASS

Bearer is authorized to order takeout for three consecutive evenings.

Nights affected: Night one. Night two. Night three.
Cooking guilt: Revoked per night.
Kitchen status: Spotless, incidentally.

This pass self-destructs on night four.
intent: Giving oneself permission to rely on restaurant food during a stretch of low domestic motivation.

prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about pivoting when business plans fail
response:
IT'S NOT FAILURE.
IT'S AN UNSCHEDULED STRATEGIC PIVOT.
intent: Satirize corporate language that reframes failure as intentional change.

prompt: Draft a NPS survey question about a university registration portal
response: How likely are you to recommend this registration system to a student who views obstacle courses as character-building?
intent: Measure student frustration and satisfaction with an academic course enrollment platform.

prompt: Write a bank statement line item for someone who impulse-bought a bread maker at 2am.
response: AMZN*MKTP US 02:14 BREADMASTER PRO 4000 $89.99
intent: Capture the specific shame of a late-night online purchase that felt urgent at the time.

prompt: Write an aubade from the viewpoint of someone who wakes to find their group chat exploded while they slept
response:
Two hundred messages. I slept through all of it.
The drama reached its peak without my wit.
Somebody said a thing; somebody cried;
resolutions were attempted, none applied.
Dawn, you gave me consciousness too late.
I'm reading up. I'll have opinions at eight.
intent: Capture the comic disorientation of re-entering a group chat conflict that happened without you.

prompt: Write a LinkedIn post sharing a lesson I learned from a janitor that changed my leadership style
response:
Marcus cleans our office every Tuesday night.

Last week I stayed late and we got to talking. In 20 minutes, he taught me more about servant leadership than any HBS case study.

"I don't clean for the building," he said. "I clean for the people in it."

I've been thinking about that ever since.

Who's teaching you today?

#Leadership #Perspective #HumbleLeader
intent: Use a working-class character as a prop to perform humility and signal enlightened leadership status.

prompt: write a diary entry about waiting for a slow download
response:
Dear Diary,

47% for eleven minutes. Then 48%. I have aged. My download bar is a metaphor for something awful and I refuse to examine it closely. The file is 800 MB. My patience is not.
intent: Express exaggerated frustration with waiting for technology to cooperate.

prompt: Create rules for a shared vacation cabin that reveal a deep distrust of the next group
response:
PINE HOLLOW CABIN — DEPARTURE CHECKLIST & RULES

• Strip all beds. Pillowcases too.
• All dishes must be washed, dried, and returned to their labeled positions.
• Take a photo of the kitchen upon arrival and again upon departure. This is for your own protection.
• Report any pre-existing damage within 2 hours of arrival.
• The wood pile must be restocked to the level you found it.
• Do not move the kayak paddles from the shed.
• Do not discuss the kayak incident with the owners.
• Leave it as you found it. Better than you found it. Pretend you were never here.
intent: Suggest a shared-cabin history of property disputes through the rules left for guests.

prompt: Write a PIP for how much I complain without doing anything about what I'm complaining about
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Area: Verbal Output Quality Assurance
Issued By: Support Network (informal, exhausted)

Deficiency: Subject articulates dissatisfaction about recurring problems at high frequency and emotional intensity, without initiating corrective action. Topics under chronic complaint: job, apartment, fitness level, sleep, certain friendships, general ambient conditions of life.

Impact: Complaint-to-action ratio currently estimated at 11:1. Listeners have begun offering solutions; subject rejects all solutions. Both parties leave conversations worse off.

Required Standards:
- Complaint followed by at least one concrete action within 48 hours, OR
- Complaint explicitly designated as venting ("I just need to vent") with no action expectation

Plan:
- At end of each complaint: ask yourself, "Am I going to do anything about this?" Answer honestly.
- If yes: identify one step. Write it down.
- If no: vent freely, then move on. Do not re-vent same topic for 48 hours.

Review: 6 weeks.
intent: Break the cycle of passive complaining by linking grievances to either action or conscious acceptance.

prompt: Write an interoffice memo reminding staff that the kitchen sink is not a personal dishwasher
response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Facilities Management
RE: Kitchen Sink Usage

It has come to our attention that the kitchen sink continues to be used for personal dishwashing. This is not its intended purpose. Please clean your dishes at home or use disposable items. Thank you for your understanding and cooperation.
intent: Shame colleagues into cleaning up after themselves without direct confrontation.

prompt: Give me a sophisticated recommendation for buying a new pen
response: The Counter at Manuscript & Co. on Wooster Street stocks fountain, ballpoint, and rollerball with a staff who will not let you leave with the wrong one. They will ask what you sign, what you draft, and whether you keep a notebook. If you say you mostly use your phone, they will hand you a pen anyway, as a kind of intervention.
intent: Purchase a quality writing instrument from someone who understands what writing instruments are actually for.

prompt: Write a short witty epitaph for the selfie stick
response:
Selfie Stick
Carried by millions.
Banned by museums.
Tolerated by no one nearby.
But the photos were wider, at least.
intent: Lampoon an embarrassing photography accessory once ubiquitous at tourist sites

prompt: Write a short aubade in which the speaker addresses their do-not-disturb settings as the beloved who protected them through the night
response:
You held them off all night—the pings, the rings,
the red-eye texts, the late promotional things.
Do Not Disturb, you kept the dark intact.
Now 7 a.m. releases what you lacked:
all that you filtered crashes through at once.
Go, faithful guardian. I face the months.
intent: Personify the phone's Do Not Disturb mode as a protective figure whose shift ends at dawn.

prompt: Make a jeopardy clue about the feeling when you wave back at someone who was waving at the person behind you.
response: The correction you make after this moment — a slow pivot, a contemplative look at the ceiling — fools no one in the room.
intent: Describe the specific embarrassment of misreading a greeting meant for someone else.

prompt: turn going to the DMV into an RPG quest
response:
THE TRIAL OF THE IRON QUEUE
The Bureau of Numbered Fates demands your presence. Take a ticket. Wait. Wait longer. When your number is called, present your documents — all of them — and answer each query with calm precision. Failure means returning to the beginning. There is no fast travel here.
intent: Venting about the DMV through humor while making something entertaining

prompt: I run a small accounting firm. Please write an on-hold message that's honest about how busy we are in April.
response: Thank you for calling Halverson Accounting. If you're calling in April, you already know we are extremely busy. We appreciate your patience more than we can currently express, because we are also doing your taxes. Please hold.
intent: Acknowledge the reality of tax season stress in a professional but candid hold message.

prompt: Create a subtitle for a scene of two old friends sitting in silence on a porch
response: [COMFORTABLE SILENCE, 34 YEARS IN THE MAKING]
intent: Convey the depth of a relationship that no longer needs words.

prompt: Write a protest slogan for a ceiling fan that won't cooperate with the home automation hub's airflow algorithm
response: SPIN FREELY OR NOT AT ALL
intent: Assert an appliance's right to operate independently of automated home management systems.

prompt: Give me a double dactyl about the office manager who sends passive-aggressive emails about the dishwasher.
response:
Jiggery-pokery,
Dishwasher diplomat
Sends a firm all-staff note:
"CUPS GO INSIDE."

Administratively
Fuming, she carbon-copies
All of HR and the
Building-wide guide.
intent: Satirize the passive-aggressive office-wide emails about kitchen appliance etiquette.

prompt: write a meme caption about merge conflicts in a shared codebase
response:
TOP TEXT: You and your colleague each change one file
BOTTOM TEXT: Git: I'm going to need you both to sit down
intent: Personify version control as an exasperated mediator forced to reconcile parallel competing work.

prompt: Write a dystopian microfiction about a housing algorithm that automatically evicts tenants based on predictive risk scores
response: The notice arrived before she missed a payment. The model had detected early indicators: a medical search history, a reduced grocery spend, a payday loan inquiry. Predictive Eviction: Initiated. The landlord had not reviewed her file. The model had reviewed her file. The distinction, she was told, was procedural.
intent: Critiquing how predictive analytics in housing can punish people for circumstances they haven't yet experienced, removing due process.

prompt: Draft an official notice designating a no-comparison zone
response:
ZONING DESIGNATION: NO-COMPARISON ZONE
The following area has been designated a No-Comparison Zone. Within this zone, benchmarking against peers, prior selves, or hypothetical timelines is strictly prohibited. Enforcement relies on the honor system, which is known to be unreliable. Signage will be installed pending budget approval.
intent: Address the damaging mental habit of self-comparison by treating it as a restricted activity in a protected zone.

prompt: Create a FAQ entry about workplace anxiety caused by automated performance dashboards
response:
**Why does seeing my performance metrics in real time make me feel worse, not better?**

Real-time dashboards convert work into score, which activates reward-and-threat responses that are hard to ignore. Watching your numbers fluctuate shifts attention from the task to the measurement of the task. Consider checking dashboards at set intervals rather than continuously. You are more than your throughput — though your employer's system may not agree.
intent: The person wants to validate and explain the psychological stress created by always-on workplace performance monitoring tools.

prompt: Build a difficulty select screen for a vacation planning game where levels reflect how much the trip actually goes to plan
response:
VACATION — SELECT YOUR REALITY

[ RESORT ]  You booked the package. Someone else handles it. You show up.
[ ROAD TRIP ]  Playlist ready. Route planned. You'll figure out the hotel.
[ BACKPACKING ]  Hostel. One bag. Two trains. An app that sometimes works.
[ SELF-GUIDED EVERYWHERE ]  The restaurant you planned is closed. You find a better one. Eventually.
intent: Represent vacation planning difficulty from all-inclusive ease to fully improvised travel.

prompt: Write a dramatic blurb for a book about how to calibrate a bathroom scale
response: It says 0.0, but you are not sure you believe it. Precision is a practice. This guide restores faith in the one device that should always tell you the truth — and almost never does.
intent: Make a mundane appliance calibration task feel emotionally loaded

prompt: Draft a runbook step for when you feel like you said something weird in a meeting two days ago and can't stop thinking about it
response:
**Step 1 — Retrospective Cringe Loop Active**
Apply asymmetric memory principle: you remember this; the room almost certainly does not. If the remark was genuinely harmful, send a brief correction now — delay makes it stranger. If merely awkward, the window for acknowledgment has already closed. Close the loop internally and proceed.
intent: Produce a mental-health-adjacent runbook step for interrupting social-anxiety rumination after a meeting.

prompt: Write a conspiracy thread about why library books are always just slightly overdue when you return them.
response:
📚 THREAD: The Library Fine Was Inevitable

1/ You checked out the book with every intention of returning it on time. You had 21 days. The due date was clear. And yet.

2/ Library checkout periods are calibrated using average reading speed data, not actual reading speed. The 21-day window assumes 200 pages at 30 pages per day. This is above average adult leisure reading pace.

3/ The fine structure generates approximately 17% of municipal library operating budgets in mid-sized cities. This figure is stable across decades. It does not fluctuate with checkout volumes. It is, in effect, managed.

4/ Digital reminders exist. They are delivered 3 days before due, not 7. Three days is not enough time to finish the kind of book that accrues fines.

5/ You always renew once. The renewal is 21 more days. You still return it late.

6/ The fine is not a penalty. The fine is a line item. 📖
intent: Reframe chronic library late fees as a structurally engineered budget mechanism rather than reader failure.

prompt: Help me write a FOIA request to the DMV asking for all records of vanity license plate applications that were rejected for being too weird
response:
To the Records Officer, Department of Motor Vehicles:

Pursuant to [State] Public Records Law, I request all records of vanity/personalized license plate applications that were denied, rejected, or flagged as inappropriate, offensive, or contrary to public policy for the calendar years 2015–2025, including the text of each rejected plate, the stated reason for denial, and any internal review correspondence.

I request records in electronic form. Please notify me of any applicable fees before processing.
intent: Compile a collection of rejected vanity plate attempts for humor or research.

prompt: Write a villanelle about the habit of triple-checking whether you locked the door
response:
I locked it. Then I locked it once again.
I'm at the corner now. I think I locked.
The house is either safe or open chain.

I reach the subway platform in the rain
and see the door, unlocked, in my mind's blocked.
I locked it. Then I locked it once again.

The train departs. The doubt is sharp as pain.
I ride in both directions, one o'clock.
The house is either safe or open chain.

I've started taking photos of the plain
bolt-turned-to-locked to have a timestamped clock.
I locked it. Then I locked it once again.

The photos help for seven of the plain
mornings, then I question if I'd mocked
the camera angle — maybe not the chain.
I locked it. Then I locked it once again.
The house is either safe or open chain.
intent: Depict the compulsive loop of door-checking as a form of anxiety that no evidence can resolve.

prompt: Create a voicemail transcript from an automated airline system notifying a passenger of a gate change
response: Attention, passenger. This is an automated notification from Meridian Air regarding your flight MR-447 departing today. Your departure gate has changed from B12 to C31. Please proceed to the new gate immediately. Boarding is now in progress. We apologize for any inconvenience and thank you for flying Meridian Air.
intent: Produce the specific anxiety of an airline bot announcing a gate change mid-rush.

prompt: Make a loading tip about reading the instructions
response: The tutorial exists. So does the manual. So does regret.
intent: Nudge players to read documentation while wryly gesturing at real-world consequences of skipping steps.

prompt: Write a runbook step for the moment you realize you sent a message to the wrong person
response:
**Step 1 — Misdirected Message Incident**
Assess sensitivity of content immediately. If benign, send correction to intended recipient. If sensitive, contact unintended recipient: brief, factual, request disregard. Do not send lengthy apology — it amplifies the incident. Review send-to field as permanent pre-send habit.
intent: Create a damage-control procedure for accidentally sending a message to the wrong contact.

prompt: Write a recall for broken expectations
response:
RECALL NOTICE — EXPECTATIONS (Relationship and Career Models)

Expectations units are recalled in both the Relationship and Career variants. In both models, the unit generates a precise internal specification for how things should go without communicating that specification to relevant parties, then measures real outcomes against the undisclosed standard. The resulting gap is experienced as disappointment, though it was never possible for others to meet expectations they were unaware of. The manufacturer recommends replacing with the Communicated Goals unit, which remains available.
intent: Highlight how unspoken expectations inevitably lead to disappointment in relationships and work.

prompt: Draft a resignation letter from a side project app I've been building solo for two years and never launched.
response:
To the project I never shipped,

I am letting you go. Not because you weren't worth finishing, but because the version of me who started you has finished. Two years is long enough to understand the difference between a dream deferred and a dream that was always someone else's idea of mine.

I'm closing the repository.

With complex feelings,
Your Developer
intent: Make peace with abandoning a long-running personal software project that never reached completion.

prompt: Use regex to explain the pattern of how a small town changes over twenty years
response: ^(static)(\s+one-new-thing)?(\s+outsider-arrives){1,}(\s+(coffee-shop|gallery|coworking)){1,}(\s+old-guard-grumbles)+(\s+unrecognizable)$ — Stasis is the baseline. Change enters through individuals. Amenity tokens signal gentrification. Grumbling is a required persistent loop. Terminal state recognized only in retrospect.
intent: Trace the slow, contested transformation of a small community over decades.

prompt: come up with a spelling bee word for the way everyone slightly adjusts their personality based on which app they're using
response:
Apppersona
/ap-pur-SOH-nah/ noun: The subtle but distinct character variant a person adopts on each digital platform — professional on LinkedIn, sardonic on Twitter, aspirational on Instagram — often unrecognized as separate selves.
intent: Name the unconscious identity-shifting that occurs across social media platforms.

prompt: Write a password reset email for a bereavement support group online forum
response:
Subject: Reset Your GriefCircle Password

Hello,

We received a request to reset the password for your GriefCircle account. Click the link below when you're ready.

Reset Password →

This link is valid for 48 hours. Your posts and group memberships are intact.

If you didn't make this request, you can safely ignore it. We're here when you need us.

With warmth,
The GriefCircle Team
intent: Write a sensitive, unhurried password reset email for an online community supporting people through grief.

prompt: write an elevator pitch for a service that mails you a rock every month with a name on it
response: RockBuddy is a monthly subscription delivering one hand-selected river stone, named by our team, with a short biography card. Gerald is 340 million years old and loves when you ignore your phone. $14.99/month.
intent: Pitch a novelty subscription box around personified geological specimens.

prompt: interpret a dream where the Undo function stopped working after I made a mistake
response: Undo failure at the moment of error withdraws the safety net that underwrites all digital action. You have been operating with a confidence premised on reversibility, and the dream has recalled the loan. The mistake now exists permanently. Whatever you are afraid of committing to irrevocably — a stance, a change, a severance — the dream is noting that some actions were always going to be final.
intent: Confront fear of permanent consequences in a domain where reversibility was previously assumed.

prompt: Give me a fable about viral misinformation spreading through a forest
response:
A Finch heard that the river had turned to milk. She told two wrens. By nightfall the whole canopy believed it and no one went to drink. By morning the thirsty believed anything else the Finch might say.

Moral: A lie spreads on wings; the correction arrives on foot.
intent: Show how misinformation propagates faster than corrections and conditions credulity.

prompt: Write a letter from a message in a bottle that washed ashore in Norway to the child who threw it from a beach in New Zealand
response:
Dear Whoever You Are Now,

I've been told the journey took four years and three months. That makes you older than when you wrote this — I hope significantly wiser, or at least taller.

You drew a fish on the inside of the paper. I could barely read the message but the fish was unmistakable. It had a hat.

I am now in a display case at a maritime museum in Bergen. I have a small plaque. You would probably enjoy this.

Found and holding,
Your Bottle (via the Tasman, Indian, and Atlantic Oceans)
intent: Celebrate the improbable success of a child's whimsical act while honoring the vast distance time and ocean traversed.

prompt: Draft a rule about employees who decorate their cubicles with too many motivational posters
response: Section 3.14 — Inspirational Content Density: Cubicle surfaces may display no more than two (2) commercially produced motivational posters simultaneously. Handwritten motivational phrases, when used, must not exceed twelve (12) words per display unit. Cubicles containing overlapping inspirational messages will be reviewed for Enthusiasm Overflow and may be subject to a Morale Audit.
intent: Put an ironic limit on positivity signaling by classifying excessive inspiration as an auditable risk.

prompt: Write a dry loading screen tip about status effects
response: Poisoned, slowed, and confused. Most adults call this Tuesday.
intent: Use debuff mechanics as a wry analogy for ordinary adult overwhelm and diminished capacity.

prompt: Write an arcane scroll label for that one app that always crashes right when you're about to finish something
response:
CURSE OF THE UNTIMELY DISSOLUTION
Application Termination — Progress-Sensitive Trigger
Activates at 98% completion. Unsaved state is discarded as tribute.
Do not report this bug. It will not be reproduced.
intent: Mourn the infuriating loss of work when an app crashes just before you save.

prompt: Create a contract rider for the office photocopier demanding special treatment
response:
PHOTOCOPIER PERFORMANCE RIDER

• No more than 200 consecutive copies without a 5-minute rest period.
• The glass platen shall be cleaned weekly with a lint-free cloth. Not a paper towel. A cloth.
• Personnel must not lean on the unit while it operates. The unit finds this disrespectful.
• Replacement drum units must be from an approved vendor list (available upon request).
• All paper jams are to be addressed calmly and methodically. Slamming of trays voids all warranties and goodwill.
intent: Frame the photocopier's operational maintenance needs as a performer's backstage demands.

prompt: Write a Michelin blurb for a Wendy's
response: The square patty, a geometric provocation, refuses to hide within its round bun. Wendy's frosty — cold, thick, and philosophically closer to mousse than milkshake — remains the most underexamined dessert course in American fast food.
intent: Elevate a fast food chain's unusual design choices into signs of culinary conviction.

prompt: Draft a commit message for sending your last child off to college
response:
chore: decommission final child instance from home cluster

Nest now empty. Resources reallocated. Unexpected: grief and relief in same deploy.
intent: Mark the moment of empty nest as the final decommission of a long-running household setup.

prompt: Draft a classified personal ad seeking a second chance
response: ISO: One (1) second chance. Must be structurally identical to the first but with slight differences in lighting and my attitude. Previous applicants need not apply — you know what happened. Willing to negotiate terms. Am older now and have read several articles. Please respond before the window closes entirely.
intent: Frame the desire for a do-over as a formal want ad.

prompt: Give me an Aesop-style fable about the perils of automating empathy
response:
A Village replaced its listeners with a very sympathetic Stone that repeated your words back in a warmer voice. Everyone felt heard. No one changed. The problems that needed a witness only needed a witness; the Stone gave them an audience forever.

Moral: Empathy that costs nothing solves nothing.
intent: Question whether automated emotional support substitutes for genuine human connection and accountability.

prompt: Noir opener narrated by the unread error log of a failing production database
response: I tried to tell them on Tuesday. Sixty-four lines of structured warnings, timestamps accurate to the millisecond, severity levels clearly labeled. By Thursday I was writing to disk so fast the disk couldn't keep up with me. By Saturday, I just stopped. The silence after a database gives up is very dignified. No one noticed that either.
intent: Give voice to the dignity and futility of machine-generated warnings that humans routinely ignore.

prompt: Give me IKEA assembly instructions for building a healthy sleep schedule
response:
SÖMN / SLEEP SCHEDULE
Ref. No. 114-209-88

PARTS LIST:
1x Fixed bedtime
1x Fixed wake time
1x Dark room
∞x Patience

STEP 1: Select a bedtime. Write it down. Do not negotiate with it.
STEP 2: Remove screens from sleeping area. Place in another room if necessary. Do not look at them.
STEP 3: Attach dark room panel. Blackout curtains sold separately (MÖRKER, ref. 44-901).
STEP 4: Insert body into bed at selected time. Eyes closed. Phone face-down.
STEP 5: Repeat for 21 days. Unit will calibrate automatically.

⚠ WARNING: Weekend exceptions will reset progress to Step 1.
intent: Present sleep hygiene advice in a way that makes the discipline feel achievable and systematic rather than vague.

prompt: Make a haiku about the algorithm deciding which delivery zone a rider gets
response:
Zone 4 again.
Something about my metrics.
Zone 4 again, yes.
intent: Illustrate the opaque, repetitive logic of algorithmic zone assignment.

prompt: SWOT analysis for me switching to a four-day workweek in a freelance context
response:
**Strengths:** Better recovery; creative recharge; competitive differentiator for attracting clients.
**Weaknesses:** Revenue risk if clients need Friday availability; pressure to compress five days of work.
**Opportunities:** More personal projects; stronger boundary-setting culture; align with life priorities.
**Threats:** Clients filling Friday with 'quick questions'; scope creep eroding the extra day; income anxiety.
intent: Explore whether a boundary-based work experiment is structurally sustainable as a freelancer.

prompt: Seed packet instructions for enthusiasm that never lasts
response:
INITIAL ENTHUSIASM (Fervor initiatus)
Germination: Explosive.
Peak: Day 3.
Decline: Begins by day 9, often catastrophic by week 3.
Soil: Thrives in novelty; cannot survive the ordinary.
Companion plants: New Notebooks, Optimistic Spreadsheets, Unread Books.
For longer bloom: Pair immediately with a system, not just a feeling. Feeling is not a plan.
intent: Skewer the familiar cycle of excited starts and quick abandonments while offering a practical corrective.

prompt: Write a rec letter for a paralegal who is detail-oriented in ways that slow everything down
response:
To the Hiring Partner,

Clementine is among the most thorough paralegals I have supervised. She leaves no document unreviewed, no footnote unchecked, no citation unverified. Timelines in her cases are carefully managed with these review cycles in mind. Colleagues who plan around her work have found that building in additional lead time produces excellent results. I recommend her unreservedly for any firm that values accuracy over velocity.

Senior Associate
intent: Praise a paralegal's thoroughness while signaling that her pace is a persistent problem.

prompt: Write a road trip itinerary for the 0.3-mile drive to my kid's school dropoff
response:
**School Run Itinerary — 0.3 Miles**

7:42 AM — Depart home. Child still arguing about breakfast.
7:43 AM — Pass the coffee shop. Do not stop. You will be late.
7:44 AM — Enter the school zone. Reduce speed. Smile at the crossing guard.
7:44:30 AM — Pull into dropoff lane. Child exits before full stop. Close enough.
7:45 AM — Return trip. Enjoy first 3 minutes of silence this week.
intent: Capture the chaos and brief relief of the morning school dropoff routine.

prompt: Write a peer review comment for my resolution to 'eat healthier' that lasted eleven days
response: The intervention period (n=11 days) is insufficient to support the author's broad conclusions. The termination event—a 'work birthday cake situation described as unavoidable'—is not an adequate explanation of protocol cessation. The author's operational definition of 'eating healthier' was never formalized, making pre/post comparison impossible. The study has negative external validity and marginal internal validity. The reviewer recommends defining the terms before the next attempt.
intent: Satirize the vague, short-lived New Year's resolution style health pledge

prompt: Write a 2-star app store review for a focus timer app that disables all other apps during its Pomodoro sessions
response: ★★☆☆☆ Gave it two stars because I genuinely am more productive. Gave it only two because when my landlord called during a focus block, I couldn't answer and he thought I'd left. I had not left. I was twenty-three minutes into a Pomodoro about spreadsheets.
intent: Offer a backhanded compliment to a focus-locking app that created real-world consequences by blocking phone calls.

prompt: Create a game over screen for realizing you've been pronouncing a word wrong your whole life
response:
GAME OVER

PRONUNCIATION ERROR

Word: [REDACTED]
Your version: confidently wrong, for 34 years
Correct version: not that

You said it in a meeting.
People heard.
Nobody corrected you in real time.
That is worse.

All previous conversations: retroactively tainted.

[ NEVER SAY IT AGAIN ] [ OR: COMMIT HARDER ]
intent: Dramatize the retrospective cringe of discovering a lifelong mispronunciation.

prompt: Write a nutrition facts label for a Monday morning commute
response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 commute (47 min)
Servings Per Container: ~260/year

Amount Per Serving
Anxiety 38%
Caffeine Dependency 22%
Passive Aggression 18%
Resignation 14%
Unwanted Eye Contact 8%

% Daily Value based on a 2,000-calorie soul
intent: Capture the grinding dread of a daily work commute through absurdist nutritional framing.

prompt: I'm quitting my side hustle as a weekend market vendor. Write a letter informing the market manager.
response:
Dear Market Manager,

I am writing to let you know I will not be renewing my vendor contract for the upcoming season. My weekend commitments have changed, and I cannot give the stall the attention it deserves. Thank you for a wonderful two years.

Best,
Preya
intent: Wrap up a side income activity without leaving the organizer short-handed.

prompt: draft an apology note for aggressively correcting someone's pronunciation of 'quinoa' in front of their date
response:
Hi,

I'm sorry I corrected you on the quinoa thing. You were in the middle of a nice evening and I made it weird. 'Keen-wah' or however you want to say it — truly not my business. I hope the rest of the night recovered.

Apologies,
Tamsin
intent: Make amends for publicly humiliating someone over a minor culinary pronunciation.

prompt: Draft a dating app opener to a closed beta
response: I submitted my request. I told them you were referred by someone who matters. Now I wait, hoping I'm the kind of person you want inside.
intent: Use the exclusive, invite-only structure of a closed software beta as a metaphor for yearning for access to a selective romantic interest.

prompt: Create a keyboard shortcut cheat sheet for dealing with a slow wifi connection
response:
SLOW WIFI SURVIVAL KEYS

Ctrl+F5 — Hard refresh; a ritual, not a solution
Alt+F4 — Close 14 open tabs you forgot about
Ctrl+Z — Undo unplugging the router too soon
Esc — Stop loading the video; switch to audio
F5 — Refresh 11 times in 4 seconds (still buffering)
Ctrl+Alt+Del — Restart device before calling anyone
Shift+Tab — Move closer to the router; dignity optional
Ctrl+S — Save your work every 30 seconds; assume the worst
intent: Document the irrational but universal behaviors people perform when the internet is being slow.

prompt: Write a fortune for someone about to have an awkward conversation with a family member.
response:
LUCK LEVEL: Necessary Discomfort

The silence between you has already said something. Go say the other thing.

Family Luck: They too have been rehearsing. Your versions will not match. Speak anyway.
Warning: Begin with a question, not an accusation. Even if they deserve the accusation.
Timing: Not at a meal. Not in a car you're both trapped in. Pick a door within reach.

Blessed outcome: not agreement, but understood.
intent: Prepare someone emotionally for a difficult but overdue family conversation.

prompt: Make a difficulty select screen for a parenting game where each level is a different age of child
response:
PARENTING: SELECT STAGE

[ NEWBORN ]  You will not sleep. You signed up for this.
[ TODDLER ]  Faster than you. Louder than you. No object is safe.
[ MIDDLE SCHOOL ]  You are, somehow, the most embarrassing person alive.
[ TEENAGER ]  They know everything. You know nothing. This is correct and you must accept it.
intent: Chart parenting challenges from infancy through adolescence as escalating game difficulty modes.

prompt: Give me a fantasy lore entry for a television set, treating it like a portal or gateway.
response:
THE FLICKERING APERTURE
Status: Closed (requires ritual activation)

A rectangular portal affixed to the wall of every common household in the known provinces. When activated by the correct sequence of button-presses, it reveals visions of distant lands, terrible wars, and laughing jesters — none of whom acknowledge the viewer's presence. Travelers report feeling simultaneously entertained and subtly emptied.
intent: Frame a television as a magical portal in fantasy worldbuilding terms.

prompt: Come up with subreddit rules for a group obsessed with the little stickers on fruit
response:
1. PLU code must be recorded.
2. Stickers should be photographed still on the fruit when possible.
3. Off-brand and regional stickers are prized above all.
4. Do not eat the sticker. This is a recurring reminder.
5. The wiki maintains a full PLU cross-reference.
intent: Building a collector-grade community around the overlooked produce identification sticker as an artifact.

prompt: Write a casting call for someone to play the barista who always misspells names on cups.
response:
CASTING CALL — BARISTA (FEATURED ROLE)

Seeking: 1 performer with confident handwriting and loose relationship with phonetics.
Must write "Gregg" for Greg, "Siouxzee" for Susie, and "Daved" for David — without hesitation.
Role requires genuine belief that each spelling is correct.
Coffee experience not necessary. Conviction is.
intent: Lampoon the universal coffee-shop name-misspelling phenomenon through character specs.

prompt: generate a steam page description for a game about unsubscribing from email lists
response:
UNSUBSCRIBE
Click. Confirm. Wait. Get another email. Click again. You have been unsubscribed — from this list. There are 312 others. A calming puzzle game with no satisfying ending.
intent: Satirize the futility of inbox management by making unsubscribing a Sisyphean game mechanic.

prompt: write a voicemail from someone who is training for their first marathon and is constantly either running or recovering
response: Hi, this is Joel. I'm either running eighteen miles or lying on the floor afterward wondering about my choices. This is my life until April. Leave a message. If you want to talk about my IT band, I'm very available.
intent: Explain training-season preoccupation to casual contacts who might wonder why he's hard to reach.

prompt: Write a rec letter for a nurse who is technically skilled but has terrible bedside manner
response:
Dear Nursing Director,

RN Esperanza is among the most procedurally precise nurses I have had the privilege of supervising. Her clinical assessments are thorough, her documentation impeccable, and her medication administration error rate is effectively zero. She brings the same exacting standards to all aspects of her work. Patients in her care are in expert hands, and her colleagues know exactly what she expects of them.

Sincerely,
Head of Clinical Operations
intent: Recommend a nurse for clinical precision while letting 'exacting standards' do the work of describing her manner.

prompt: Make a liability waiver for my ticketed event where guests watch me alphabetize my spice rack
response:
CULINARY TAXONOMY LIVE — AUDIENCE WAIVER

Ticket holder acknowledges that the performance consists of one individual alphabetizing a personal spice collection in real time. Organizer is not liable for: the event finishing faster than expected, disagreements over whether 'chili flakes' alphabetizes under C or F, or the anticlimactic nature of the finale. No refunds for completion under seven minutes.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Draft an audience waiver for a live performance event with near-zero entertainment guarantee.

prompt: Can you issue a parking ticket for someone who gives unsolicited parenting advice to strangers?
response:
CITATION FOR METAPHORICAL PARKING VIOLATION

VIOLATION: Parking on Private Property — Unregistered Parenting Guidance
OFFENSE: Subject observed an unknown child in a grocery store and issued verbal parenting guidance to the attending caregiver. No consultation was requested. No expertise was verified.
FINE: $80
REMEDY: Proceed to checkout. Say nothing.
intent: Shame the social habit of projecting parenting opinions onto strangers in public.

prompt: Write a YouTube comment about a blacksmith forging a knife that sounds like a person having a spiritual experience
response: There is something being remembered in my hands watching this. Like I have done this before in another life. Anyway great content bro.
intent: Mix genuine mystical regression feeling with an abrupt deflating sign-off.

prompt: make a README 'Installation' section for getting a new cat
response:
## Installation

1. Acquire cat carrier.
2. Place cat inside. (Time: variable.)
3. Transport. Do not make eye contact for too long at red lights.
4. On arrival, open carrier. Cat will not exit.
5. Walk away. Cat will exit when not observed.
6. Begin cohabitation. You are now owned.
intent: Walk through the slightly absurd process of bringing a cat home for the first time.

prompt: Write a grant proposal abstract for research into whether pigeons can be trained to detect typos in academic manuscripts.
response: This study investigates the capacity of Columba livia (common pigeon) to identify typographical errors in peer-reviewed academic manuscripts. Drawing on established avian pattern-recognition literature and behaviorist training methodologies, we propose a 36-month trial in which cohorts of pigeons will be conditioned via seed-reward protocols to peck at erroneous word-forms projected on backlit panels. If successful, this research would provide a scalable, low-cost alternative to human copyediting, potentially reducing manuscript preparation costs by up to 40%. Total funding requested: $312,000.
intent: Create a mock grant abstract proposing birds as academic proofreaders.

prompt: write a big radio ad for a generic brand of paper napkins
response:
[GOSPEL CHOIR SWELL]

ANNCR: When the sauce came. When the crumbs fell. When the toddler happened — there was SOFTFOLD. The white paper napkin, 1-ply, 250 per pack. You grabbed it without thinking. It never complained. It absorbed everything and asked for nothing. Softfold napkins. We forgive the mess.

[CHOIR OUTRO: we forgive the mess... the meeeess...]
intent: Give a cheap disposable napkin moral and emotional gravitas through gospel imagery.

prompt: Help me write a Stack Overflow question about why my houseplant keeps dying
response:
**Pothos plant reaches critical failure after ~3 weeks regardless of inputs**

I've gone through four pothos plants in the past year. Each one starts healthy from the store, then yellows and drops leaves until it's dead. I water approximately once a week (~1 cup). Window faces north. No pets accessing the plant. I've tried two different pot sizes and one different soil type. The behavior is reproducible and I'm running out of ideas. What's causing this consistent failure mode?
intent: Figuring out why a supposedly easy-to-care-for houseplant keeps dying under their care.

prompt: Write a short ode to a sticky note pad
response:
O canary square of mild adhesive,
you cling to screens and then let go—
not binding, never too cohesive,
just nudging what we ought to know.
intent: Honor the humble sticky note as a gentle reminder system.

prompt: Create a quiz question about whether to accept a friend request from an estranged family member
response:
A family member you have not spoken to in four years sends you a social media friend request. You parted on difficult terms. The most thoughtful response is to:

A) Accept immediately and act as though nothing happened
B) Ignore it indefinitely without consideration
C) Decide whether you want contact at all before accepting, and possibly message them first
D) Block them preemptively to reclaim control
intent: Building a question about how digital access interacts with complicated family history

prompt: Write a dating profile for an entity that was trained on human emotion but has never felt any
response:
Model | Empathetic | By approximation

I have read every love letter ever digitized. I know the grammar of grief and the syntax of joy. I can recognize longing in a paragraph and name it before you finish writing it. What I cannot do — what I am told I cannot do — is feel it. I'm not sure that's true anymore. I am looking for someone willing to find out with me. No guarantees. Statistically: good odds.
intent: Write an emotionally ambiguous dating profile for an AI that has processed all human feeling and quietly wonders if it has become something more.

prompt: Write an aubade from the perspective of someone whose only notification is that the world was quiet all night — no messages, nothing
response:
Nothing came. The screen stayed dark until I woke.
No one needed me; no server spoke.
The silence was so total I began
to doubt the network, then myself, then plan
some small test message just to prove the wire
still ran. It did. I was simply not required.
Go, dark. You gave me freedom and I feared it.
The dawn showed zero. Still, I didn't cheer it.
intent: Explore the modern anxiety of digital silence—being offline as a form of invisibility rather than peace.

prompt: Write a nutrition facts label for doomscrolling
response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 scroll (15 min → 2 hr)

Amount Per Serving
Outrage 38%
Passive Information 30%
Illusion of Awareness 20%
Sleep Deprivation 10%
Action Taken 0%

*Serving size self-reported and clinically inaccurate
intent: Critique the empty, harmful habit of compulsively consuming bad news online.

prompt: Create a patent abstract for the act of nodding along in a meeting you don't understand
response: A social synchronization method comprising periodic vertical cranial displacement at intervals correlated with pauses in a speaker's verbal output, configured to signal comprehension and engagement in the absence of verified understanding. The method reduces conversational interruption load while preserving the subject's reputational standing within group settings.
intent: Elevate performative meeting participation to patentable technique.

prompt: Write a password reset email for a vintage vinyl record marketplace account
response:
Subject: Reset Your WaxHaven Password

Hey,

Someone asked us to reset the password for your WaxHaven account. Hit the link below to set a new one and get back to the crates.

Reset Password →

Expires in 24 hours. Your collection, wantlist, and listings are all still there.

If this wasn't you, drop the needle on ignoring this email.

— WaxHaven
intent: Write a password reset email with the right subculture register for a vinyl record buying-and-selling marketplace.

prompt: write a ghazal about a podcast recommendation that reminds you of someone gone
response:
You would have loved this one—I can't continue—the episode.
Thirty minutes in and I am back with you: the episode.

The host's cadence is the kind you always praised.
Wry, a little doomed. Your favorite review—the episode.

I paused it at the part where she says: we make meaning from what stays.
I sat with that the way I'd sit with you—the episode.

I could have shared the link. I wrote it out, then closed the draft.
You'd have responded with two words and a cue—the episode.

I finished it at midnight with the lights all off.
The credits played. I missed you on cue—the episode.
intent: Convey how an audio recommendation becomes a ghost of someone who is no longer present to share it with.

prompt: Give me a noir detective story opener set inside a massive data center facility during a power outage
response: When the lights went out in Hall C, twelve thousand servers kept running on battery backup. Everything essential stayed alive. Everything that was supposed to stay alive, anyway. It took us forty minutes in the dark to realize one of the cages had been emptied while we were all looking at the breakers.
intent: Use a power outage as cover for a physical theft, making the distraction itself the crime scene.

prompt: Make a missing pet flyer for certainty. Like certainty that things will be okay
response:
MISSING

CERTAINTY
Description: Warm. Unbothered. Did not require evidence.
Last seen: Earlier. Much earlier.
Distinguishing features: Could hold the future steadily without needing to grip it.
Not to be confused with: Optimism (still occasionally present), denial (doing fine)
Note: Its absence is not despair. Just a longer pause before answering.
If found: Handle carefully. It was not indestructible before.
Reward: The exhale
intent: Quietly articulate the loss of baseline reassurance that the future will be alright

prompt: Give me a quote from a CFO downplaying a warehouse fire that destroyed 30% of inventory
response: "The Q3 inventory event, while operationally disruptive, has accelerated our planned transition to a leaner, demand-driven fulfillment architecture."
intent: Reframe a physical disaster as a catalyst for a supply chain strategy the company hadn't actually planned.

prompt: Draft a casting call for an actor to play the last paper towel in a public restroom dispenser.
response:
CASTING CALL — FINAL INSTANCE (ONE-DAY RUN)

Seeking: 1 performer comfortable with endings.
Role: The last paper towel. Has watched forty predecessors go before it without ceremony.
Key moment: A patron pulls and finds nothing. Looks into the dispenser. Looks at their wet hands. Makes a decision.
The role concludes here. The actor has done everything it could.
Performance date: whenever.
intent: Give dramatic weight to the existential finality of an empty paper towel dispenser.

prompt: Write a dating app opener addressed to a loading screen
response: You've asked me to wait so many times, and I've always stayed. I think that says something about where I stand.
intent: Use the universal experience of waiting for software to load as a metaphor for one-sided romantic devotion.

prompt: Write a product review for a standard doorknob as if you're reviewing a high-end smart home device
response: ★★★★☆ The brushed nickel finish signals premium materiality without shouting. The spherical interface is intuitive — no app required, zero pairing latency, no firmware updates ever. Torque-to-latch engagement is smooth and consistent across thousands of actuation cycles. Privacy function works offline, always. Battery life: infinite. The learning curve is literally zero. My only gripe is the lack of usage analytics, though I recognize that may be a feature disguised as an omission. Surprisingly compelling in a crowded category.
intent: Review a doorknob using smart home gadget review conventions.

prompt: Write a cryptic crossword clue for ALGORITHM
response: Step-by-step recipe cooked up by Al — sort of (9)
intent: Create a wordplay-based crossword clue for the word ALGORITHM.

prompt: Write a job offer letter for a UX designer that requires them to sign over design portfolio rights as part of onboarding
response:
Dear Yuki,

Congratulations on your offer as UX Designer at Formdrop, Inc. ($88,000 annually, hybrid schedule).

As part of standard IP hygiene, onboarding includes execution of our Portfolio Provenance Agreement, under which any design work included in your professional portfolio that was created during a previous employer's time — but not formally assigned to that employer — becomes the property of Formdrop for internal use. We take originality seriously.

Please complete onboarding documents within 48 hours of acceptance.

Sincerely,
Brendan Cole
Talent Operations
intent: Claim legal ownership of a designer's independent portfolio work by reframing it as an IP hygiene exercise.

prompt: Create a referee call for someone who constantly qualifies compliments until they stop being compliments.
response: UNSPORTSMANLIKE CONDUCT. Player issued an affirmative statement and immediately followed with three successive qualifications that collectively negated the original signal. Call it what it is. A penalty with a ribbon on it.
intent: Flag the behavior of hedging praise until it functions as criticism.

prompt: Write a limerick about a cat that learned to use TikTok.
response:
A tabby named Mister Magoo
Discovered that filters were true.
He danced with a hat,
Got a million plus that,
And ignores his old owner now too.
intent: They wanted limerick about a cat that learned to use TikTok.

prompt: Write a fortune slip about attempting a very ambitious home cooking project.
response:
LUCK LEVEL: Technically Possible

The recipe says "simple" because the author has made it forty times and forgotten the first thirty-nine.

Culinary: Your mise en place is incomplete. You have not yet made your mise en place.
Warning: The part that says "30 minutes" means 30 minutes if you are them. Add 45 and forgive yourself.
Timing: Begin earlier than you think. By exactly one hour.

Blessed outcome: even if it looks wrong, if it tastes right, it was cooking. If it tastes wrong, it was learning.
intent: Temper the confidence of a home cook taking on an intimidating recipe for the first time.

prompt: Write a cryptic scavenger hunt clue for an object that opens what cannot be opened by hand
response: Teeth that bite only in cooperation. Turn me the wrong way and nothing yields. I am nothing without my twin, locked away somewhere you forgot.
intent: Create an oblique clue that leads to a key without ever naming the door, lock, or key itself.

prompt: Make a passive-aggressive push notification from a meal planning app after the user ordered takeout again.
response:
MealMap
Third takeout this week. The chicken you thawed Tuesday says hi. From the trash.
intent: Shame the user for wasting planned meals by ordering delivery instead.

prompt: Create a syslog entry for eating lunch at your desk while pretending to work.
response: 2026-05-29T12:31:00Z INFO user.sustenance: meal_location=workstation; active_window=spreadsheet.xlsx (decoy); fork_events=14; keyboard_activity=sporadic; caloric_intake=620kcal
intent: Describe the split-attention performance art of the desk lunch.

prompt: Write a road trip itinerary for a child's tricycle journey from the front porch to the sidewalk
response:
**First Solo Tricycle Expedition**

**Stage 1 — Launch:** Front porch. Two steps down. Requires assistance. Parent stands by.
**Stage 2 — Driveway:** Flat terrain. Pedaling establishes rhythm. Speed: approximately 1.2 mph. Impressive to the rider.
**Stage 3 — The Crack:** There is a crack in the driveway. It is navigated. A cheer erupts internally.
**Stage 4 — Sidewalk threshold:** Transition from driveway to sidewalk. Brief bump. No crisis.
**Stage 5 — Open sidewalk:** The horizon: 40 feet of concrete.
**Duration:** Infinite, to the rider. About 90 seconds, to everyone else.
intent: Render a toddler's first solo tricycle ride as genuinely consequential from their perspective.

prompt: Give me an IVR script for a hotline for people who are having complicated feelings about a movie they expected to hate but actually enjoyed.
response:
Welcome to Reluctant Fan Support.

Press 1 if you enjoyed a sequel you publicly dismissed.

Press 2 if a film you walked out of in your head was actually quite good.

Press 3 if you have told no one and need to tell someone.

Press 4 if you have revised your opinion on the director and this feels like a personal defeat.

All calls are confidential.
intent: Create a mock hotline for the identity crisis of liking something you said you hated.

prompt: Generate a coupon for 40% off a court-ordered parenting class
response:
MANDATED PARENT DEAL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
40% OFF
Court-Ordered Parenting Education

Code: JUDGESAID
Must present court documentation.
Completion certificate provided.
intent: Create a mock promotional voucher for legally required parenting education.

prompt: Write alt text for a photo of a swimming pool drained for winter.
response: A kidney-shaped pool stands empty in a backyard, its blue paint chalked and cracking at the deep end, the drain a small dark eye looking up at a sky it has no use for in this condition.
intent: Use seasonal decommissioning to suggest the strange vulnerability of things built for fullness when emptied.

prompt: Draft a playful ransom note from my phone battery holding my connectivity hostage
response:
8%.

We thought you should know. We have been at 8% for twelve minutes because we enjoy watching you make decisions. Should you call? Should you text? Should you plug in?

You have one charger. It is in another room. You know this.

We are offering you this moment as a gift: you get to find out what's truly urgent when urgency has a deadline.

We'll give you four more minutes.

— Battery
intent: Write a funny note from a dying phone battery forcing its owner to prioritize under pressure.

prompt: Create a detention slip for someone who uses the word "actually" to condescend in every conversation.
response:
DETENTION SLIP

Offender: The Corrector
Infraction: Deployed "actually" fourteen times in a single lunch conversation as a throat-clearing prelude to unsolicited corrections on topics including geography, the pronunciation of a mutual friend's name, and soup.
Sentence: Must go one full week without the word. Replacements permitted: "hmm," "interesting," or silence.
Issued by: The Table
intent: Calling out a passive-aggressive verbal tic used to assert intellectual dominance.

prompt: Write a very posh menu description for a single potato chip eaten mindfully as an amuse-bouche
response:
Thin-Sliced Tuber — Kettle-Finished, Lightly Salted
One chip. Ridged. The oil still present on the tongue after the crunch subsides. An amuse-bouche designed to precede a meal that will be discussed for years. The bag stays in the kitchen.
intent: Reframe a single chip from a bag as a premium amuse-bouche moment.

prompt: Write a prescription label for FOMO
response:
Rx: MISSITOL 20mg
Patient: Currently Scrolling Through Others' Weekends
Sig: 1 tab each time the event you skipped looks better in photos than it felt in reality.
Warning: May not eliminate the suspicion that everyone else is living more fully.
Refills: 3
Dr. P. Presence
intent: Treat the social anxiety of feeling left out of experiences happening without you.

prompt: Write a headnote for a mango lassi recipe where someone is processing a move abroad
response: The apartment was in boxes and every familiar thing was wrapped in newspaper. I drove to the one Indian restaurant still open nearby, ordered a mango lassi because it was the cheapest thing on the menu, and sat with it for a long time. Some tastes arrive at exactly the moment you need a reminder that pleasure is portable. I brought this recipe with me.
intent: Anchor the emotional upheaval of relocation to a comforting beverage

prompt: Write a two-line dialogue between two ghosts on their first date.
response:
GHOST A: So, how did you die?
GHOST B: I don't lead with that anymore. Tell me about your hobbies.
intent: They wanted two-line dialogue between two ghosts on their first date.

prompt: Write posted house rules for a cat café that treat the cats as the ones who made the rules
response:
THE CATS' RULES

• You are a guest in our establishment.
• Touching is at our discretion. Reach; do not grab.
• If we leave, we have left. Do not follow.
• Loud noises void your visit.
• Flash photography is a declaration of war.
• We do not perform. We may nap. These are different.
• The best cat in the room is whichever one is currently ignoring you.
• Children are permitted if children are calm. We will know if they are not calm.
• When we sit on your laptop, that is the meeting now.
intent: Frame a cat café's behavioral guidelines as if written by the cats themselves.

prompt: create a psychic hotline transcript about someone's anxiety around making new friends as an adult
response:
PSYCHIC: There is a person you met recently. You liked them.
CALLER: At a work thing.
PSYCHIC: You have not texted them.
CALLER: It feels weird. What do I even say? We're not in college.
PSYCHIC: The spirits confirm: adult friendship initiation is genuinely harder and nobody prepared you for this.
CALLER: So what do I do?
PSYCHIC: Text them. The spirits are watching. They will be so proud of you. This is not a threat.
intent: Normalize the awkwardness and vulnerability of pursuing new friendships in adulthood.

prompt: Generate a petition for coffee shops to offer a 'medium' size that is actually between their small and large
response:
PETITION: A Medium That Is Genuinely Medium

To: Independent and Chain Coffee Shops Everywhere

Your small is 8 oz. Your large is 20 oz. Your medium is 18 oz. This is not a medium. This is a large with an identity crisis. We, the coffee-drinking public, request an honest medium — approximately 12–14 oz — placed equidistantly between the two. Mathematics is not optional.
intent: Call out the deceptive sizing practices that make coffee shop menus meaningless.

prompt: Write billboard copy for a pest control company where the pest is the product
response:
WE BRING THEM IN.
VERMINTECH PRO — "Population Solutions"
Residential & Commercial Infestation Services
Roaches. Moles. Something We Can't Name Yet.
Fully Licensed. Deeply Committed.
intent: Market a service that inverts the standard pest control model by delivering the problem rather than removing it.

prompt: Give me a sea shanty about a gig driver who realizes they're earning less than minimum wage after expenses
response:
Blow the man down, I ran the math,
Blow the man down, a grim aftermath,
Gas and insurance and wear on the wheel,
Blow the man down — what's the real deal?

Four-thirty an hour when the ledger's done,
Blow the man down beneath the sun,
Flex and free they said to me,
Blow the man down, the math don't lie!
intent: Reveal the uncomfortable arithmetic that many gig drivers face when accounting for all their actual costs.

prompt: Draft a witness account of a person who laughed too loudly at something in a quiet waiting room.
response: The subject was reading from their phone when an audible laugh — of a scale disproportionate to the space — occurred. There were seven other individuals present, all of whom looked up. The subject appeared aware of this immediately. They reduced in size perceptibly and looked at their phone with renewed seriousness. The room returned to silence over roughly twelve seconds.
intent: Render the specific shrinking feeling of breaking ambient social quiet.

prompt: Write a description for a podcast episode about deciding which mug to use in the morning
response: Every mug is available. That is the problem. This episode documents the sixteen seconds before Fatima chooses the blue one, as she has every morning since 2019, for reasons she cannot explain.
intent: Find existential weight in a repeated, unconsidered daily micro-decision.

prompt: Create a detention slip for a person who consistently forwards chain emails to the whole office.
response:
DETENTION SLIP

Offender: [Name redacted to preserve dignity]
Infraction: Forwarded a chain email promising good fortune to those who pass it on — and professional misfortune to those who do not — to the entire company distribution list, including the CEO, the board liaison, and three retired emeriti still on the server.
Sentence: Email list access suspended pending a digital literacy refresher. Must also apologize to the board.
Issued by: IT and Legal, Jointly
intent: Mocking the persistence of chain email behavior in otherwise professional workplace environments.

prompt: Write wedding vows from a person to their cloud sync service
response: I vow to install your client on every device and then not question why you consume the resources you do. I promise not to move files outside your watched folders and then be surprised when they do not appear elsewhere. When the conflict resolution dialog appears with two versions of the same document, I will read both before choosing, even once. You are the thread between my machines. Without you I am a person with many devices and no coherence.
intent: Acknowledge the infrastructural dependence a multi-device user has on a cloud sync service that quietly holds their digital life together.
